retrieved_docs.title: Papillary Thyroid Carcinoma -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Papillary thyroid carcinoma (PTC) is an epithelial malignancy showing evidence of follicular cell differentiation and distinctive nuclear features. It is the most frequent thyroid neoplasm and carries the best overall prognosis. The tumor usually appears as an irregular solid mass but may have cystic features in rare cases. One key feature of PTC is its ability to invade adjacent structures like lymphatics. About 10% of patients may present with metastatic disease at initial presentation. The overall prognosis is good for most patients, especially the ones younger than 45 years of age.

retrieved_docs.title: Papillary Thyroid Carcinoma -- Complications -- Local or Regional Recurrences
retrieved_docs.content: Local or regional recurrences occur in 5 to 15% of patients with PTC. [49]

retrieved_docs.title: Papillary Thyroid Carcinoma -- Complications -- Distant Metastases
retrieved_docs.content: Distant metastasis occurs in only 1% to 25%, mainly in the lungs and bones. Other less common sites are the brain, liver, and skin. [50]

retrieved_docs.title: Papillary Thyroid Carcinoma -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Papillary thyroid carcinoma (PTC) is an epithelial malignancy showing evidence of follicular cell differentiation and distinctive nuclear features. It is the most frequent thyroid neoplasm and carries the best overall prognosis. The tumor usually appears as an irregular thyroid solid mass, but it may have cystic features in rare cases. One key feature of PTC is its ability to invade adjacent structures like lymphatics. About 10% of patients may present with metastatic disease at initial presentation. The overall prognosis is good for most patients, especially the ones younger than 45 years of age. This activity illustrates the role of the interprofessional team in diagnosing and managing patients with papillary cancer of the thyroid.

retrieved_docs.title: Papillary Thyroid Carcinoma -- Treatment / Management -- Surgery
retrieved_docs.content: A lobectomy is an option for unifocal tumors smaller than 4 cm with no evidence of extrathyroidal extension or lymph node metastasis. For patients with PTC >4 cm, or with gross extra-thyroidal extension (clinical T4), or clinically apparent metastatic disease to nodes (clinical N1) or distant sites (clinical M1), the initial surgical procedure should include a near-total or total thyroidectomy and gross removal of all primary tumor unless there are contraindications to this procedure. If surgery is chosen for patients with thyroid cancer <1 cm without extra-thyroidal extension and cN0, the initial surgical procedure should be a thyroid lobectomy unless there are clear indications to remove the contralateral lobe. Thyroid lobectomy alone is sufficient treatment for small, unifocal, intrathyroidal carcinomas in the absence of prior head and neck radiation, familial thyroid carcinoma, or clinically detectable cervical nodal metastases. [26] [33] Prophylactic central-compartment neck dissection (ipsilateral or bilateral) should be considered in patients with PTC with clinically uninvolved central neck lymph nodes (cN0) who have advanced primary tumors (T3 or T4) or clinically involved lateral neck nodes (cN1b) or if the information be used to plan further steps in therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Papillary Thyroid Carcinoma -- Histopathology -- Gross Findings
retrieved_docs.content: Grossly, PTC typically presents as an invasive neoplasm with poorly defined margins, a firm consistency, and a granular white-cut surface. Calcifications may be present. The size is widely variable, with a mean diameter of 2-3 cm. PTC has 3 classification categories based on the size and extent of the primary lesion. Minimal carcinoma or occult carcinoma/micro-carcinoma tumors are 1.5 cm or smaller and show no evidence of invasiveness through the thyroid capsule or cervical lymph nodes. These lesions are typically nonpalpable and usually found as incidental findings during operative or autopsy examination. Intra-thyroid tumors are greater than 1.5 cm in diameter but remain confined to the thyroid gland with no evidence of extra-thyroid invasion. Extra-thyroid tumors extend through the thyroid capsule to involve the surrounding viscera. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Papillary Thyroid Carcinoma -- Complications -- Extra-thyroidal Extension
retrieved_docs.content: Extra-thyroidal extension occurs in 8% to 32% of the cases. [48]

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Nodule -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis for thyroid malignancy will greatly vary depending on the histological type and subtype of cancer in addition to several individual characteristics, including age at diagnosis, size of the primary tumor, presence of soft tissue invasion, or distant metastasis. [26] [27] Most patients with papillary thyroid cancer do not die of the disease. One case series on patients with non-metastatic papillary thyroid cancer showed malignancy-related mortality of 6%. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Papillary Thyroid Carcinoma -- Staging
retrieved_docs.content: Several staging systems have been proposed for PTC and continue to evolve. [42] [43] The most commonly used are: For patients less than 45 years: Stage l: any Tm, any N, M0 (cancer localized to the thyroid) Stage ll: Any T, any N, M1 (cancer spread to cervical nodes or distant organs) For patients older than 45 years: Stage l: T1, N0, M0 (cancer less than 2 cm) Stage ll: T2, N0, M0, and T3 (cancer localized to the thyroid that is between 2 to 4 cm) Stage lll: T4, N0, M0, and any T, N1, M0 (lesion more than 4 cm and spread limited to the neck) Stage lV: Any T, any N, M1 (cancer spread outside the neck and to distant organs)

retrieved_docs.title: Papillary Thyroid Carcinoma -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Evaluate the risk factors associated with the development of papillary thyroid cancer. Identify the different features seen in the histopathology report of a biopsy in a patient with papillary thyroid cancer. Assess the different treatment choices for papillary thyroid cancer based on tumor size and other clinical features. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Papillary Thyroid Carcinoma -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: PTC usually presents as an asymptomatic (painless) thyroid mass with or without enlargement of regional (cervical) lymph nodes. Hoarseness and dysphagia occur in approximately 20% of cases, signaling recurrent laryngeal nerve involvement with vocal cord paralysis or tracheal compression. Nodal metastases in the lateral neck are reported in 27% of patients at presentation, most often originating from tumors in the ipsilateral thyroid lobe. [19] In rare cases, there could be lateral lymph node involvement. [20] The examination reveals a painless hard lesion, usually less than 5 cm. The mass is often fixed and has irregular borders.

retrieved_docs.title: Papillary Thyroid Carcinoma -- Treatment / Management -- Adjuvant Radioiodine Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: After thyroidectomy, radioiodine is the therapy of choice in patients with PTC to ablate residual normal thyroid tissue. Radioiodine therapy is indicated in several cases. [34] These include: Patients with a tumor >2 cm and 1 of the following risk factors: gross extra-thyroidal extension, age > 45 years, lymph node and distant metastases Patients with a tumor <2 cm and distant metastasis

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Nodule Biopsy -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Papillary thyroid cancer rates have been gradually increasing in incidence. [23] Papillary and follicular cancers are considered differentiated malignancies and are treated with total or partial thyroidectomy. [24] In particular, identifying papillary thyroid cancer is especially important due to its relatively good prognosis, with mortality rate, which have been reported as low as 2%. [25] Treatment includes surgical resection or administration of radioactive iodine. Anaplastic thyroid carcinoma, however, is extremely aggressive and occurs during the 6th and 7th decades. Clinically, anaplastic thyroid carcinoma commonly results in aggressive tumor expansion and growth, leading to compressive symptoms from mass effect on adjacent structures such as the cervical airway. The 5-year survival rate is around 5%. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Anaplastic Thyroid Cancer -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Anaplastic thyroid carcinoma is a rare, aggressive malignant tumor accounting for 2% to 3% of all thyroid gland neoplasms. In contrast to differentiated thyroid malignancies such as papillary and follicular thyroid cancers, anaplastic thyroid cancer is undifferentiated. Furthermore, anaplastic thyroid cancer is highly locally invasive, with a propensity for early lymph node positivity and distant metastatic disease.

retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of neck from primary malignant neoplasm of thyroid is morphologically associated with a primary malignant tumor (ورم خبيث أولي). In addition, سرطانة توتية (papillary carcinoma) is classified as a primary malignant tumor, indicating that it is a subtype of ورم خبيث أولي. These relationships underscore that papillary carcinoma, as a primary malignant tumor, can give rise to metastatic disease in cervical lymph nodes, and that the metastatic lesion shares the morphological characteristics of the original primary malignant tumor.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 933
retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure is identified as the anatomic site associated with cancer of the thyroid. Thyroid cancer is clinically associated with the procedure استئصال الدرقية (thyroidectomy). Thyroidectomy, whether performed as a partial or complete operation (thyroidectomy; partial or complete), is a targeted procedure for the head and neck structure and serves as the parent procedure for both استئصال الدرقية (full thyroidectomy) and استئصال الدرقية الجزئي (partial thyroidectomy). The partial thyroidectomy (استئصال الدرقية الجزئي) frequently co‑occurs with دراق, reflecting a contextual relationship. Understanding these interconnections is essential for planning appropriate surgical management of thyroid cancer within the head and neck region.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 890
retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure serves as the anatomic site associated with thyroid cancer. Thyroid cancer is classified under the broader category of thyroid disorders (اضطراب صماوي) and is clinically linked to the surgical removal of the thyroid gland. Surgical management of thyroid disease includes thyroidectomy, which may be performed as a complete removal (استئصال الدرقية) or as a partial removal (استئصال الدرقية الجزئي). Both complete and partial thyroidectomy are sub‑procedures encompassed by the general procedure termed “thyroidectomy; partial or complete.” The head and neck region is therefore the target anatomy for these thyroidectomy procedures. Additionally, partial thyroidectomy frequently co‑occurs with the anatomical reference “دراق,” reflecting its relationship to the thyroid gland itself. Understanding the connections among the head and neck anatomy, thyroid cancer, underlying thyroid disorders, and the spectrum of thyroidectomy techniques is essential for accurate diagnosis, treatment planning, and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2412
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of the neck originating from a primary malignant neoplasm of the thyroid, primary malignant neoplasm of the head of the pancreas, and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are all instances of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, which are pathological processes characterized by uncontrolled malignant cell growth. Each condition represents a distinct organ‑specific cancer that arises as a primary tumor (thyroid, pancreatic head, thymus) and, in the case of the thyroid cancer, can spread to regional lymph nodes in the neck. Recognizing these entities as primary malignant neoplastic proliferations highlights their shared nature as serious pathological processes that require comprehensive oncologic assessment, staging, and targeted therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 872
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of neck from primary malignant neoplasm of thyroid, primary malignant neoplasm of nasal bone, and primary malignant neoplasm of thymus are all examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, which are pathological processes. Each represents a malignant tumor arising in a specific anatomic site—thyroid cancer that has spread to cervical lymph nodes, a malignant growth originating in the nasal bone, and a malignant tumor arising in the thymus. Recognizing these entities as primary malignant neoplastic proliferations highlights their aggressive, disease‑causing nature and underscores the need for thorough oncologic assessment and appropriate therapeutic management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1831
retrieved_docs.content: Head and neck structure is identified as the anatomic site associated with cancer of thyroid, indicating that thyroid malignancies arise within this region. Cancer of thyroid is classified as a type of اضطراب صماوي (endocrine disorder), linking the disease to broader hormonal system pathology. The primary therapeutic intervention for thyroid cancer is thyroidectomy; partial or complete, a surgical procedure that is clinically associated with استئصال الدرقية (the Arabic term for thyroidectomy). This procedure is also recognized as the target anatomy–procedure relationship for the head and neck structure, reinforcing its role in the management of thyroid disease. The synonym relationship between thyroidectomy; partial or complete and استئصال الدرقية further clarifies that both terms refer to the same surgical treatment. Together, these connections underscore the anatomical, pathological, and therapeutic links among head and neck structures, thyroid cancer, endocrine disorders, and thyroidectomy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1146
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of the thyroid is classified as a subtype of اضطراب صماوي (thyroid disorder) and is clinically associated with استئصال الدرقية (thyroidectomy). The broader procedure category thyroidectomy; partial or complete serves as a parent concept for both استئصال الدرقية (total thyroidectomy) and استئصال الدرقية الجزئي (partial thyroidectomy). Partial thyroidectomy frequently co‑occurs with دراق (the thyroid gland) itself, reflecting the close anatomical and therapeutic relationship between the gland and its surgical removal. This network of relationships underscores that thyroid cancer is viewed within the spectrum of thyroid disorders and that its management commonly involves either total or partial thyroidectomy, each linked to the underlying thyroid tissue.

In terms of clinical associations, “kanker; metastase” is clinically associated with “سرطان الغدة الدرقية” (thyroid cancer), highlighting that patients with thyroid malignancies often present with or are at risk for metastatic spread. Consistent with this, “سرطان الغدة الدرقية” is classified as a “ورم صماعي خبيث” (malignant neoplasm), confirming its status as a primary malignant tumor. Finally, “kanker; metastase” is classified as “نقائل إلى المبيض” (metastasis to the ovary), indicating that ovarian involvement is a recognized classification within the broader metastatic cancer category. Overall, the knowledge base depicts a network in which metastatic malignant neoplasms are organized by both site of origin (thymus, endocrine gland) and site of spread (respiratory/intrathoracic organs, skin, ovary), with specific cancers such as thyroid carcinoma linked clinically to this metastatic framework. This integrated hierarchy aids clinicians in understanding the pathways

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1139
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic cancer (referred to as “kanker; metastase”) is classified as a specific form of malignant neoplasm that lacks a defined primary site, making it a child of the broader category “malignant neoplasm without specification of site.” This metastatic entity can arise in several organ systems, including the skin (سرطان الجلد النقيلي), the respiratory and intrathoracic organs, and endocrine glands such as the thymus. The metastatic disease of the thymus is a more specific subclass of metastatic malignant neoplasms of endocrine glands, indicating that thymic involvement represents a particular manifestation of endocrine‑gland metastasis. Clinically, metastatic lesions are recognized as a key finding that signals the presence of both thymic metastatic disease and unspecified malignant neoplasms. The metastatic process is closely linked to thyroid cancer (سرطان الغدة الدرقية), which itself is a type of malignant tumor (ورم صماعي خبيث). Across these entities, the term “النقيلة” (denoting malignancy) is repeatedly associated, underscoring the malignant nature of the primary and metastatic neoplasms. Together, these relationships highlight that metastatic cancers can present in diverse anatomical locations, are identified through characteristic lesion findings, and are intrinsically connected to other malignant tumors such as thyroid carcinoma, emphasizing the importance of comprehensive evaluation and management of metastatic disease in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 239
retrieved_docs.content: Carcinoma of the head of the pancreas is described with the morphology term “الورم السرطاني” and is directly mapped to the broader category of cancer of the pancreas. Metastatic carcinoma to a lymph node of the head and neck is classified as a type of “الورم السرطاني” (inverse isa) and its specific morphology is identified as “السرطان النقيلي”. The metastatic malignant neoplasm that involves lymph nodes of the neck originating from a primary malignant neoplasm of the thyroid represents a primary malignant neoplastic proliferation and is characterized by the morphology “ورم خبيث أولي”. Primary malignant neoplasm of the head of the pancreas also exhibits the morphology “ورم خبيث أولي”, is a primary malignant neoplastic proliferation, and is mapped to cancer of the pancreas. Primary malignant neoplasm of the nasal bone shares the same primary malignant morphology “ورم خبيث أولي” and is likewise a primary malignant neoplastic proliferation. The term “سرطانة توتية” is an inverse isa of “ورم خبيث أولي”, indicating it is a specific subtype of primary malignant tumor. Finally, primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus is identified as a primary malignant neoplastic proliferation. Together, these relationships clarify how various pancreatic, head‑and‑neck, thyroid‑derived, nasal‑bone, and thymic malignancies are categorized by their pathological processes and morphological descriptors, supporting accurate diagnosis, coding, and patient‑centered management.
